author: @burtybasset4486
text: Great vid! I'm a big fan of pedal power!! Not for any climate change reasons, but one thing I was concerned about at the beginning of lockdowns was expecting this virus having plague like consequences and electrical energy production stopping. I've been looking through a few pedal powered metal lathe videos and there aren't many on YT. Maybe because they require relatively large flywheels to maintain relatively consistent RPM's? There are a few simple wood powered ones mind, powered by a springy branches etc which are pretty cool.

author: @pedjamilosavljevic6235
text: I (kind of) remember , when I was a child , there were some human power machines , like grinding wheel and some speial equipment (I don't remember it clearly , I was only small) , besides sawing machines. But I distinctly remember some of them had flywheel , because they kept on going , even when the operator left the machine (I would try to stop it and usually they shouted at me to move away) . I believe that flywheel , attached to your machine , would give more mechanical advantage , after getting up to speed and would make smoother operation. It would , probably , add complexity to the power train (couple of sprockets and mounting brackets and additional chain and flywheel).

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it would indeed mate

author: @thornhedge9639
text: That's the scroll saw I mentioned yesterday.  Good job Robert!   There used to be many belt drive gadgets that could be used this way sold through catalogs like Sears many years back.  Then it was basically one little motor, many attachable tools.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: they still do them in a 'modern' form

replies.author: @gcewing
replies.text: The modern version does this with batteries!

replies.author: @thornhedge9639
replies.text:
@gcewing 
  The problem is that they rely too heavily on technology which is massively prone to failure, whereas the old pulley driven machines are still around and as solid as ever (Though they're disappearing fast).  All this modern,(Improved materials) is slated for the garbage heap before  it leaves the factory. 
  Modern manufacturing tolerances are less than desirable at best and intentionally sloppy at worse.
  Lets not even get started on batteries......
  My remarks here are not directed at you Greg Ewing; they are just my commentary on the sad demise of everything of  importance and an obvious reminder that rather than progressing we are going backwards rapidly.   Just as we cannot today build the structures that are left from previous civilizations, so our children will not be able to build even what our grandfathers did.
  I love Robert; he's a scrapper, and incredibly inspiring in his creativity and enthusiasm, but make no mistake even he couldn't replace the mighty machining lathes and steel shaping and forming machines our grandfathers used to build the world we've inherited.
  We are less than they were and that is not an admirable legacy to hand down...

author: @totherarf
text:
Watching these videos I can't help but be impressed!

With light duty devices (like this saw) I did notice a variance in speed. With your blender that is not relevant but with a saw it might be. Have you considered a flywheel? It would even out the irregularities even if it is not strictly necessary for a proof of concept like this!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I have mate - nd I think you are right for PoC I don't need it but for finished device it is essential

author: @NorthernKitty
text:
This project has had me looking at ways people have been using "pedal power" in modern times.  I saw two really creative projects where they're being used for recycling (a few puns there!).  One machine would break up and pulverize electronic circuit boards and had a neat mechanism for separating the metal from non-metal particles, and another would unravel threads from clothing to wind the thread up into skeins for re-use in knitting or weaving.   I thought the latter idea was especially cool, as I remember "pedal-powered" sewing machines, so it kinda brings textiles "full circle" (yet another opportunity for a pun!)

It's truly amazing to see what these are being used for in modern times that I was completely unaware.  It truly is a "swiss-army knife" of a device!!  😊  You constantly amaze me - just when I think you've introduced us to the most incredible use of a simple technology, you find something else that's even more incredible!  😄

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: lol - it Is pretty amazing how inventive people are
